context.description: A reflective prose passage interprets Gilgamesh's transformation as inner self-overcoming driven by pain, shame, and the collapse of false motives. It generalizes a principle of motivation: people act when the pain of not acting exceeds the pain of acting.
normalization_notes: Interpreted as a philosophical nonfiction book page on consciousness and selfhood.
Source is German; summary translated into English.

rows.paragraph_text: The passage argues that Gilgamesh's journey exists to destroy the part of himself that seeks power and magic, turning him into a figure who freely wants to make the world better. It frames heroic transformation as forced recognition of shame, pain, and inner contradiction, and compares this to motivation arising when the pain of not acting exceeds the pain of acting. It also suggests that old-life patterns can work against themselves to produce a higher will.
rows.text_content: The passage argues that Gilgamesh's journey exists to destroy the part of himself that seeks power and magic, turning him into a figure who freely wants to make the world better. It frames heroic transformation as forced recognition of shame, pain, and inner contradiction, and compares this to motivation arising when the pain of not acting exceeds the pain of acting. It also suggests that old-life patterns can work against themselves to produce a higher will.
